items.html:
The tiny organisms that cause infection

Infections are caused by micro-organisms — living things far too small to see without a microscope. They are often called germs, and only a minority of them are actually harmful to people. Knowing the main groups helps you understand why different precautions are used at different times.

  • Bacteria: single-celled organisms. Many are harmless or even helpful, such as those in your gut, but some cause infections like urinary tract infections or Clostridioides difficile. Many, though not all, respond to antibiotics.
  • Viruses: much smaller than bacteria and need to enter a living cell to multiply. They cause colds, flu, norovirus and COVID-19. Antibiotics do not work against them.
  • Fungi: include yeasts and moulds, and cause infections such as thrush and athlete’s foot.
  • Parasites: organisms such as the mite that causes scabies, which spreads through close contact.

Colonisation, infection and how harm happens

Not every micro-organism on or in the body makes a person unwell. Understanding the difference helps you protect the people you support without becoming fearful of normal, harmless germs.

  • Colonisation: micro-organisms live on the skin or in the gut without causing any symptoms. A person can carry MRSA on their skin, for example, and feel completely well.
  • Infection: the organisms multiply and damage tissue, producing signs such as fever, redness, swelling, pain or discharge.
  • Passing it on: someone who is colonised can still spread an organism to a person who is more vulnerable, where it may cause a serious infection.

This is why standard precautions apply to everyone, all the time — you can rarely tell just by looking who is carrying a harmful organism.

items.question: Antibiotics are generally effective against which type of micro-organism?

answers.feedback: Correct. Antibiotics act against certain bacteria; they do nothing to viruses, fungi or parasites.

answers.text: Viruses such as norovirus and flu.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Viruses are not affected by antibiotics; taking them for a virus gives no benefit.
Incorrect. Antibiotics target bacteria only, and even then not every type.
Incorrect. Fungal and parasitic infections need different treatments, not antibiotics.

items.html:
Putting on and taking off PPE in the right order

Personal protective equipment only protects people if you put it on and take it off correctly. The order matters, because most contamination happens as you remove items and risk touching a dirty surface with clean hands.

  • Putting on (donning): clean your hands, then apron, then a mask or respirator if needed, then eye protection if needed, and gloves last.
  • Taking off (doffing): gloves first, as they are the most contaminated, then apron, then eye protection, then the mask — and clean your hands.
  • Between people: change single-use PPE for each person and each task; never reuse it.

Clean your hands again once all PPE is removed and disposed of as clinical or offensive waste according to your workplace policy.

Respiratory and cough hygiene

Many infections, including flu, colds and COVID-19, spread through droplets released when people cough, sneeze or talk. Good respiratory hygiene reduces how far these droplets travel and how many surfaces they land on.

  • Catch it: cover the nose and mouth with a tissue for every cough or sneeze.
  • Bin it: dispose of used tissues straight away into a waste bag.
  • Kill it: clean your hands afterwards, and support the people you care for to do the same.

If no tissue is to hand, coughing into the crook of your elbow is far better than into your bare hands. Keeping tissues, bins and hand gel within easy reach makes it much easier for everyone to follow these steps.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on doffing and cough hygiene.
items.textField: When removing PPE, take off your *gloves* first because they are the most contaminated. Good cough hygiene means you *catch* a cough in a tissue, *bin* the tissue straight away, then *clean* your hands. If no tissue is to hand, cough into your *elbow* rather than your bare hands.

items.html:
Infections you may meet in care

Some infections appear regularly in care settings, and recognising them early helps you act quickly to protect others.

  • Norovirus: causes sudden vomiting and diarrhoea and spreads very easily; thorough hand washing with soap and water is essential, as gel alone is less effective against it.
  • Flu and COVID-19: respiratory illnesses spread by droplets, causing fever, cough and aches.
  • C. difficile: a gut infection causing diarrhoea, often linked to antibiotic use, that produces spores needing soap-and-water hand washing and careful cleaning.
  • MRSA: a bacterium resistant to some antibiotics; people are often colonised without symptoms but it can cause serious infection in a wound.

Recognising and responding to an outbreak

An outbreak is usually described as two or more linked cases of the same infection in the same place around the same time. Spotting the pattern early is one of the most useful things you can do.

  • Report promptly: tell your senior or manager as soon as you notice several people with similar symptoms, such as diarrhoea and vomiting.
  • Follow control measures: these may include isolating affected people, enhanced cleaning and restricting movement between areas.
  • Reinforce basics: hand hygiene, correct PPE and prompt cleaning of spills all become even more important.
  • Support communication: visitors and staff may need clear information about the risk.

Your manager will involve the health protection team when needed; your job is to notice, report and follow the agreed steps.

items.question: Several people on your unit develop sudden vomiting and diarrhoea within a day of each other. What is your best first action?

answers.text: Report it to your senior or manager straight away so control measures can start.
answers.feedback: Correct. Early reporting of a possible outbreak lets the team isolate cases, increase cleaning and limit spread.

answers.text: Wait a few days to see whether more people become unwell.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Delay lets an infection like norovirus spread widely; prompt reporting is key.

answers.text: Rely on alcohol hand gel alone to stop it spreading.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Gel is less effective against norovirus; soap-and-water hand washing and reporting are needed.

answers.text: Move affected people together with unaffected people to keep an eye on them.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Mixing affected and unaffected people spreads infection; isolation is part of outbreak control.

items.html:
What antimicrobial resistance means

Antimicrobials are medicines that kill or slow the growth of micro-organisms, and antibiotics are the group that act on bacteria. Antimicrobial resistance, or AMR, is when these micro-organisms change so that the medicines no longer work well against them.

When an antibiotic stops working, an infection that was once easy to treat can become serious or even life-threatening. Resistant organisms such as MRSA are already found in care settings, and the more antibiotics are used, the more chances bacteria have to become resistant.

AMR matters to everyone. It is not caused by one person; it builds up across whole populations, which is why using antibiotics carefully protects people well beyond the person taking them today.

Using antibiotics responsibly

You do not prescribe medicines, but you play a real part in slowing resistance through everyday good practice.

  • Prevent infection first: strong hand hygiene, PPE and cleaning mean fewer infections and so fewer antibiotics needed.
  • Support the full course: help people take antibiotics exactly as prescribed, at the right times.
  • Never share or save: antibiotics are for one named person and one infection, never passed to someone else or kept for later.
  • Report concerns: tell a nurse or GP if an infection is not improving, rather than assuming a different antibiotic is simply needed.

Remember that antibiotics do nothing for colds, flu or most sore throats, which are usually caused by viruses.

items.question: Good infection prevention, such as hand hygiene and cleaning, helps reduce antimicrobial resistance because fewer infections mean fewer antibiotics are needed.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Preventing infection in the first place is one of the most powerful ways to reduce the need for antibiotics and slow resistance.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Preventing infections reduces how often antibiotics are needed, which in turn helps slow the spread of resistance.

items.html:
Laundry, food and the wider environment

Infection can spread through the everyday tasks of a care setting, so safe routines with laundry, food and surfaces matter as much as clinical care.

  • Laundry: handle used linen away from your body, do not shake it, and bag soiled items at the point of use. Wash infected linen on the hottest cycle the fabric allows, following your workplace policy.
  • Food: clean your hands before handling food, keep raw and cooked foods apart, store food at the right temperature and never prepare food for others if you have diarrhoea or vomiting.
  • Environment: clean frequently touched surfaces such as handrails, door handles and light switches regularly, using the correct products and colour-coded equipment.

Bringing it together: a realistic scenario

You arrive for an early shift and find that Mrs Okafor, who you support at home, was sick twice overnight and has had diarrhoea. She looks tired but is talking normally. Her daughter has left the soiled bedding in a pile on the floor.

You think through what you know. Sudden vomiting and diarrhoea suggest a possible infection such as norovirus, which spreads easily. Before anything else you put on an apron and gloves, and you plan to wash your hands with soap and water, not just gel. You bag the soiled linen where it lies without shaking it, clean the affected surfaces, and report the symptoms to your manager so advice can be sought. You keep Mrs Okafor comfortable and encourage fluids while you wait.

items.question: In the scenario, which combination of actions best protects Mrs Okafor and others from a possible norovirus infection?

answers.text: Wear apron and gloves, wash your hands with soap and water, bag the linen without shaking it, clean surfaces and report the symptoms.
answers.feedback: Correct. This combines the right PPE, effective hand washing, safe laundry handling, cleaning and prompt reporting.

answers.text: Use alcohol hand gel only and carry the bedding to the machine, giving it a good shake to loosen it.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Gel is less effective against norovirus, and shaking linen releases the virus into the air.

answers.text: Wait until the end of your shift to mention the sickness to your manager.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Prompt reporting lets advice and control measures start before infection spreads.

answers.text: Skip PPE because you know Mrs Okafor well and do not want to seem distant.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Standard precautions apply with everyone; PPE protects you both regardless of how well you know her.
